summary: Jeff Atwood critiques performance-based incentive systems using Microsoft's curve rating system as a case study. He argues that while performance metrics seem logical, making them visible to management transforms them into counterproductive tools. Drawing on research from social psychology, he presents evidence that rewards actually decrease performance, particularly for creative work. The post concludes that software development, as a creative activity, is especially harmed by reward-based motivation systems.
key_points: Performance metrics are only useful when kept private to the individual being measured; exposing them to management turns them into tools of control
Research from social psychology shows people expecting rewards perform worse than those expecting nothing, especially in creative tasks
Rewards create implicit punishments, discourage cooperation, encourage competition, deter risk-taking, and shift focus from the work itself to the reward
Microsoft's curve rating system exemplifies how well-intentioned performance systems can become demoralizing and self-defeating
Software development as a creative activity is particularly vulnerable to the negative effects of incentive-based management
money_quotes: Performance metrics are useful, but only privately to the person who is being measured.
The minute you make these performance metrics available to the manager as anything other than a sanitized average, you've turned them into a self-defeating cudgel to beat people into submission.
While rewards are effective at producing temporary compliance, they are strikingly ineffective at producing lasting changes in attitudes or behavior.
About two dozen studies from the field of social psychology conclusively show that people who expect to receive a reward do not perform as well as those who expect nothing.
For every reward, there is the lack of a reward, an implicit punishment.
Rewards undermine interest by focusing on the reward rather than the process itself.
key_insight: Incentive-based performance systems undermine the intrinsic motivation that drives quality creative work like software development.
